evidence_quotes.quote: Every consumer is vulnerable if big corporations hire a team of psychologists to make their games addictive.
To make their loot boxes addictive.

paragraphs.text: While you rightfully write in the section of problems the initiative wants to tackle that in the EU we have some of the most and far reaching regulations to protect consumers it's sadly not enough. Industries block many legislations with the argument that it would destroy a sucessfull market. In many cases it's an unregulated or underregulated market. I won't suggest that we need government control and regulation in every way but in many digital markets there was no or only few regulations that gave the industry many opportunities to expand without control. History has shown us that industries and the market as a whole will expand and grow beautifully without control but at a certain point the governments need to step in and up to prevent exploitative practices that grew and implemented themselves as industry standards. Just because they can and it leads to growth industries shouldn't be allowed to do whatever they can to fish the last cent out of the pockets of their consumers. When legislations on cigarettes started to become the norm and the industry had to adapt they did so unwillingly but sucessfull. Just because it's not allowed to smoke in busses and restaurants (in germany) it's not like the tobacco companies are going extinct. Just because people had to wear seatbelts in cars the automobil-industry or the coffin industry won't go extinct. Government protection for consumers and citizen is needed if the concerned people can't protect themselves or don't know how they can protect themselves without walking away from the market alltogether. So I would kindly ask the authors of the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) to consider including consumer protection not only for minors and easily exploited/vulnerable consumers but for every customer of the games industry aswell. Every consumer is vulnerable if big corporations hire a team of psychologists to make their games addictive. To make their loot boxes addictive. To make certain mechanics only viable if you pay more and more and more. Additionally I would kindly ask the authors of the DFA to include a protection for consumers to be able to have the possibility through technical means to user their bought products and not be denied because industries and publisher decided that they want to sell a new version or another game or brand. And even if the consumer won't buy the new product there is an increasingly harder way to get software that isn't serverbound or need to call home to the publisher/developer/software distributer. And if these entities decide to distribute or suppoert the software these bought solutions are worth nothing. If the entity goes bankrupt there is no way to use the bought product. I would like to have to opportunity to use my bought software whenever I like if I am not blocked from the service because of something that violates a certain code of conduct. And even then I would wish to be able to use the software without the means to communicate throught it with others. There are games that are only sold digitally for one platform (for example Xbox oder Playstation) if the underlying servers are shut down one wouldn't be able to plug their old console in and play these games. Just because it was to expensive for the distributer to support these plattforms or they wanted to push newer ones. With old media when games where on CD's this wasn't that much of a problem. But with the software market mostly digitally nowadays and mostly downloadcodes in retail boxes, there need to be a legislation to protect consumers from praying industries and businesses. Thank you very much for your time and consideration
